text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic repair of a duodenal ulcer perforation. A 10mm infraumbilical port is created, and a 30-degree telescope is introduced for diagnostic laparoscopy, revealing fluid throughout the peritoneal cavity. A 5mm port is placed between the left subcostal margin and the umbilicus, and a suction apparatus is used to aspirate purulent materials and fluids. The pyloroduodenal region is examined, identifying a 5mm perforation. The size is measured using 5mm instruments, followed by a thorough peritoneal lavage with normal saline.

The BOIS score is used for the evaluation of the severity of the disease. The BOIS score is based on three criterias. One is shock at the time of admission that is systolic blood pressure less than ninety millimeters of mercury and the second one is severe medical illness based on ASA level that is ASA three and four and the third one is delayed presentation that is presentation more than twenty four hours after the symptoms.

A second working port is created between the right subcostal margin and the umbilicus at the mid-clavicular line. The pelvis is visible, showing a significant amount of purulent fluid that requires thorough cleaning to prevent future complications such as pelvic abscess.

The surgeon identifies a readily available omental flap for the omental patch and places a 10 mm port in the epigastric area. A liver fan retractor is introduced, and an overview of different port positions is provided. A duo polygalactin suture is introduced, and suture bites are taken 0.5 to 1 cm lateral to the healthy margin of the duodenal ulcer for simple closure with an omental patch.

The surgeon carefully performs the second suture on the perforated peptic ulcer site, ensuring the tissue edges are healthy and come together without undue tension. The ulcer defect is reinforced with an omental patch.

Patient selection for laparoscopic repair is still disputable. There are no firm criteria for patient selection for laparoscopic approach. It is better to avoid laparoscopic repair in patients with septic shock on admission and severe cardiopulmonary comorbidities. Proton term inhibitors and H pylori eradication therapy have significantly decreased the rate of peptic ulcer disease complications. But perforation of duodenal ulcer is still quite frequent in developing countries.

The surgeon uses the third suture to close the perforation in the duodenal ulcer. A tongue of omental flap is then brought over the closed perforation and tied securely. The laparoscopic approach allows for better visualization and involves closing the perforation first, followed by securing the omental flap over it. After suturing, the needle is removed, and a final wash of the surgical area is performed.
The surgeon aspirates fluid from the pelvis and places a surgical drain in the sub-hepatic area.
